text: These are some quick tips from a case I recently performed. This gentleman has a neuroendocrine tumor in the pancreas and is undergoing a laparoscopic distal pancreatectomy.
The surgeon mobilizes the short gastric vessels and opens the lesser sac, revealing a significant amount of intra-abdominal fat, including fat covering the pancreas.
The surgeon carefully identifies the splenic vessels posteriorly, aiming to preserve them during the procedure. The splenic vein becomes visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon skeletonizes the tissue laterally around the pancreas, with the patient positioned in full decubitus with the left side up, aiding the dissection process.

The surgeon identifies the tail of the pancreas, which appears atrophic and is located distantly from the splenic hilum. The presence of significant intra-abdominal fat complicates the identification of the tumor's exact location.
In cases like this, performing intraoperative laparoscopic ultrasound can be very helpful. So that's what we did here.
The surgeon uses an ultrasound probe to identify the tumor's location more posteriorly within the pancreas gland, preparing to begin skeletonizing the surrounding tissue.

The surgeon exposes the splenic artery and vein, which have been dissected and are visible in the surgical field. Remaining attachments are severed using a harmonic scalpel. The surgeon staples the tissue proximal to the lesion, and minor oozing is controlled with Surgicel.
The spleen is visible, showing good perfusion overall, with a small area of ischemia noted at the superior pole.
